| **Cancellation** (SIGINT / `CancelledError`) | ⚡ Tasks that have already started are **waited on** so their `try/finally` cleanup runs. ⚡ Tasks not yet started are **never created** — they simply don't run. |
| **Individual task failures** | Silently suppressed — one failed task doesn't halt the rest. |
| **Over-cancellation** | Uses `Task.uncancel()` (Python 3.9+) to temporarily reset the cancellation flag so the cleanup wait isn't immediately interrupted. |

# CancelledError drain requires uncancel() in Python 3.9+

When catching `CancelledError` in a parent task to drain child tasks (letting their cleanup run), calling `await` inside the `except` block immediately re-raises CancelledError on Python 3.9+ because the `_must_cancel` flag is still set. Calling `asyncio.current_task().uncancel()` first clears the flag so the drain `await` proceeds normally. Without it, the drain never actually waits — the next `await` raises CancelledError again immediately.

This only applies to explicit-task-tracking patterns (set of running tasks + asyncio.wait). The Semaphore + gather(return_exceptions=True) pattern doesn't need uncancel because `finally` blocks always run during exception propagation regardless of the cancellation flag.
EOF
